explicit_text: My father was born in France, which was a significant stop for my family after leaving Poland. My grandfather initially moved there because he thought France was more enlightened than Eastern Europe for Jews. However, before World War II, he realized France was not as safe as he hoped, prompting the family to move further away to Australia.

explicit_text: On my mother's side, our family history is quite different; we discovered that her paternal ancestors came to Australia as convicts from England in the 1830s. This was a surprising revelation since we had thought they came freely. So, England is part of my heritage through that convict lineage.

explicit_text: I grew up in Melbourne, Australia, where my family owned a steak restaurant called the Barbecue Inn. It was a popular place serving Australian beef and some Central European dishes, thanks to a Yugoslavian pastry chef we employed. Melbourne also had a strong Jewish community, including Landsmanshaftn, which connected my family to their Polish roots.

explicit_text: I made Aliyah to Israel in 1984 when I was 25 years old, after several visits starting with a gap year in 1978. I was drawn by a strong Jewish identity and Zionist youth movements like Habonim and Hashomer Hatzair. I settled in the Western Galilee, living in the community neighborhood of Tuval, and have since become recognized as an expert in Galilean cuisine.
